value: Headline: The week-ending June 12 WPSR delivered a bullish gut-punch — an 8.3 mb crude draw that drained Cushing to 20.0 mb, already below the ~25 mb operational tank-bottom — and prices have leaned into it, with WTI settling Friday at $76.54 (+$0.69) and Brent at $80.59 (+$0.74). The story this morning is the collision of a structurally tight US hub against a fading Middle East risk premium, leaving the curve backwardated but the geopolitics deflating. Refiners are running flat-out at 96.7% utilization into peak summer demand, and the physical barrel remains scarce even as headlines try to talk the market lower. Crude & products: Per last week's WPSR (week ending June 12, 2026), commercial crude fell 8.3 mb to 418.2 mb while Cushing drew 1.61 mb to 20.0 mb — down 5.79 mb over five weeks and operating below tank-bottom minimums, a setup that keeps prompt WTI basis and time spreads volatile. Gasoline drew 0.91 mb to 214.2 mb and jet fell 1.11 mb to 45.0 mb, while distillate bucked the trend with a 1.0 mb build to 103.1 mb. Refinery utilization climbed 1.4 pp to 96.7%, led by PADD V snapping +8.10 pp to 93.4% as West Coast units came back; crude inputs rose 0.23 mb/d to 17.19 mb/d. Total petroleum stocks fell a hefty 9.4 mb to 780.5 mb — an unambiguously tight print across the board. Refining margins: Margins held firm into the strong run rates, with the 3:2:1 crack at $49.11 supported by robust product pull. The distillate crack at $55.79 remains the standout despite the 1.0 mb distillate build, while the gasoline crack sits at $45.77 and jet at $42.32 — RBOB settled at $2.9122/gal (+1c) and ULSD at $3.1508/gal (+7c) Friday. The fat distillate crack and the surprise distillate build argue for refiners to keep maximizing diesel yield, but with utilization already at 96.7% there is little spare capacity left to chase margin. Watch the regrade: jet's 1.11 mb draw against jet's softer crack hints at yield-switching room into peak travel season. Curve structure: WTI remains backwardated — M1/M2 at +$0.92 and M1/M12 at +$6.54 — a structure consistent with a sub-floor Cushing and a scarce prompt barrel. Brent's M1/M2 of +$0.54 and M1/M12 of +$6.37 echo the same tightness, if slightly flatter. The Brent–WTI spread sits at $4.05, on the narrow side, which weakens the US export arb and risks barrels staying home — a dynamic that, absent a re-widening (WTI falling or Brent rising), would build US crude and partially offset the Cushing draw story. Product curves stay steeply backwardated, with RBOB M1/M12 at $17.93/bbl and ULSD at $15.01/bbl underscoring near-term tightness. News & geopolitics: The dominant macro driver is the de-escalation in the Gulf — Brent is holding near $80 after Middle East developments eased supply fears, and reporting on a potential Strait of Hormuz reopening has bled risk premium out of the flat price even as analysts caution that oil markets need more than a peace deal to recover. Retail markers globally are softening, with Australian petrol now below pre-Iran-war levels and Taiwan's CPC set to cut domestic gasoline and diesel next week, while India's oil minister flagged cheaper crude arriving even as some Indian retail prices ticked up Rs 3/L. On the supply side, the read remains that a US–Iran deal alone won't rescue Iran's oil economy, capping how quickly barrels return. Bearish forward calls — including a $60 Brent prediction for 2027 — are gaining airtime, but they sit awkwardly against a sub-floor Cushing, 96.7% utilization, and a backwardated curve that all scream near-term tightness. The takeaway: flat price is being pulled lower by geopolitics, but the physical US market is still drum-tight — trade the divergence.
